feed.title: Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge

entry.title: Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge
entry.summary:
The project discussed involves extending TinyGo to produce RISC-V ELF binaries that run on the risc0 platform, enabling the generation of zk-STARK proofs for arbitrary programs. Initially a technical challenge to learn about the RISC-V architecture and risc0's host/guest system, the project has evolved to demonstrate a practical application: proving that a Taproot output public key was produced using a BIP-32 seed via a BIP-86 derivation path, which could be crucial in a post-quantum cryptographic scenario.
A significant part of this work focuses on a potential future scenario where a soft fork might disable the keyspend path for Taproot outputs, requiring all such transactions to go through the script path, which remains secure under quantum threats. The proof system developed can validate that a wallet's Taproot outputs were generated in compliance with BIP-86, thus providing a post-quantum safe exit strategy for those wallets. This is particularly relevant as quantum computers pose a risk to classical asymmetric cryptography by potentially deriving private keys from public data.
The tools used in this project include a modified version of TinyGo, specifically tailored for RISC-V 32-bit support required by risc0, and an implementation setup using Go utilities packaged into what's termed tinygo-zkvm. This combination processes the RISC-V ELF binary, executes it, and generates the necessary cryptographic proofs. Key components are hosted on GitHub, with repositories for [tinygo-zkvm](https://github.com/Roasbeef/tinygo-zkvm), [risc0](https://github.com/Roasbeef/risc0), and [go-zkvm](https://github.com/Roasbeef/go-zkvm), each contributing to various aspects of the system from guest program execution to proof generation.
Additionally, the project encapsulates everything necessary for generating the zk-STARK proof in another repository, [bip-32-pq-zkp](https://github.com/Roasbeef/bip32-pq-zkp). This includes the Go program defining the secret witness and constraints, along with a C FFI wrapper around the original Rust host, fulfilling the complete cycle of executing the guest program, generating a trace, and finally producing a proof. The final performance metrics indicate significant resource usage both in terms of time and memory, highlighting areas for further optimization.
Future directions for this work include improving proof efficiency through hardware acceleration and exploring recursive proof compositions to reduce blockchain space usage. These enhancements aim at making the system more practical for real-world applications, ensuring it can handle the demands of future cryptographic landscapes driven by advances in quantum computing and other technological fields.
